title: Court Ousts Opposition Leader as Turkey's Political Crisis Hits Markets
subhead: An Ankara appeals court annulled the CHP's 2023 congress and removed leader Özgür Özel, triggering a market rout, a lira record low, and predictions of an imminent rate hike. Meanwhile, Turkey's parliament united across party lines to demand Gulen's extradition, 400+ flotilla activists arrived in Istanbul after Israeli deportation, and Erdogan ordered the closure of a major Istanbul university.

sections.heading: Court Ousts CHP Leader Özel in Unprecedented Ruling
sections.body:
An Ankara appeals court on Thursday annulled the Republican People's Party's (CHP) November 2023 congress, effectively removing leader Özgür Özel and the party's entire executive committee. The court reinstated former chairman Kemal Kılıçdaroğlu, 77, who lost the 2023 presidential election to Erdoğan and was subsequently voted out as party leader. The ruling cited allegations that delegates were bribed with money, municipal positions, and jobs during the congress that ended Kılıçdaroğlu's 13-year tenure.

Özel rejected the ruling as a "judicial coup" and vowed to remain at CHP headquarters "day and night." The party appealed to the Supreme Election Board (YSK), which convened on Friday to consider the objection. On Friday, the appeals court rejected the CHP's immediate appeal, leaving the party to pursue its case at the Court of Cassation — a process that could drag on past the next election. Sabancı University political scientist Berk Esen called the ruling "unprecedented in Turkish administrative law and political history," noting it opens the door for courts to determine party leadership with no comparable example since 1946.

MHP leader Devlet Bahçeli urged both camps to seek compromise, but the ruling has already inflamed the CHP's internal rift. Kılıçdaroğlu swiftly dismissed three party lawyers who had filed appeals, and 13–14 provincial chairs reportedly contacted him to express support. Özel insisted he would not form a breakaway party, declaring: "Tenants leave, homeowners stay."
sections.whyItMatters: A court annulling an opposition party's internal leadership vote has no precedent in modern Turkish history. If upheld, it establishes that the judiciary can override internal party democracy — a tool that could be used against any opposition party. It also raises the probability of snap elections, since Erdoğan faces a term limit in 2028 and needs an earlier vote to run again.

sections.heading: Markets Rout as Political Risk Hits Turkish Assets
sections.body:
The CHP ruling triggered an immediate market selloff. The Borsa Istanbul index plunged 6% on Thursday before trading was halted. The lira hit a record low, forcing the central bank to sell billions in foreign reserves to defend the currency. On Friday, stocks partially recovered but banking shares lagged and volatility persisted.

JPMorgan issued a note on Friday predicting the central bank would hike its key interest rate to 40% and could move before its next scheduled meeting in June. The bank cited the compounding effect of domestic political instability on top of the ongoing Iran war's pressure on the lira. Inflation remains above 32%, and Vice President Cevdet Yılmaz dismissed the market turmoil as "daily developments," insisting the government remained focused on its disinflation program.

The lira's slide is compounded by the CBRT's dramatically reduced reserve buffer. The central bank sold $14 billion in US Treasuries in March alone, slashing holdings from $15.7 billion to just $1.8 billion — an 89% drawdown. Combined with a record 127-tonne gold reserve drawdown, Turkey's capacity to defend the lira against further shocks is now significantly thinner than it was three months ago.
sections.whyItMatters: Turkey was already under severe economic strain from the Iran war's impact on energy prices and capital flows. Adding domestic political instability to that mix pushes the central bank toward an emergency rate hike and leaves the lira vulnerable to further selling. The near-total liquidation of US Treasuries signals how thin Turkey's defenses have become.

sections.heading: Erdogan Shuts Down Istanbul Bilgi University
sections.body:
In a separate development on Friday, President Erdoğan issued a presidential decree revoking the operating licence of Istanbul Bilgi University, effective immediately. The private university, founded in 1996, is known for its liberal values and enrolls approximately 20,000 students from Turkey and abroad. It participates in the EU's Erasmus Mundus programme.

The decree cites a law allowing closure if "the expected level of education and training is insufficient." The university had been under a court-appointed administrator since last year, when its parent company Can Holdings was seized in a money laundering and tax fraud investigation.

Law professor Yaman Akdeniz wrote that an institution built over 30 years had been "effectively shut down overnight." Videos of student protests circulated on social media. The Council of Higher Education said students would be transferred to Mimar Sinan University, the designated guarantor institution. The closure comes amid a broader crackdown on dissent that has already jailed Istanbul Mayor Ekrem İmamoğlu — Erdoğan's most prominent political rival — on corruption charges since March 2025.
sections.whyItMatters: The closure of a major university known for its liberal values, mid-academic year and via presidential decree, is a further signal of the narrowing space for independent institutions. Combined with the CHP ruling and İmamoğlu's imprisonment, it paints a picture of coordinated pressure across political, legal, and educational institutions.

sections.heading: Parliament Unites to Demand Gulen Extradition from the US
sections.body:
In a rare show of cross-party unity, Turkey's governing AKP and opposition parties issued a joint declaration in parliament on Friday demanding the extradition of Fethullah Gülen, the US-based cleric whom Ankara accuses of orchestrating the 2016 failed coup that killed 251 people. The declaration called Gülen's activities damaging to US-Turkey bilateral relations and likened his movement to organizations such as Daesh and Al-Qaeda.

The timing is notable — it comes the same week as Erdoğan's phone call with Trump about Iran, and as Turkey seeks greater US engagement on regional issues. The declaration gives the government bipartisan domestic cover for pressing the extradition demand while keeping Washington-focused diplomacy active.
sections.whyItMatters: A unanimous parliamentary declaration on Gülen's extradition is a signal to Washington that the demand has the backing of the entire Turkish political spectrum, not just the ruling party. It also underscores that even opposition parties under legal siege are willing to align with the government on issues of national sovereignty and the 2016 coup legacy.

sections.heading: 400+ Flotilla Activists Deported to Istanbul as Turkey Becomes Transit Hub
sections.body:
Israel deported more than 400 activists from the Global Sumud Flotilla on Thursday, flying them on three chartered flights from southern Israel to Istanbul. The activists, who had been intercepted in international waters while attempting to breach the Gaza blockade, arrived wearing grey sweatsuits, some limping, flashing two-fingered salutes and chanting "Free Palestine."

Deportees described physical and psychological abuse at the hands of Israeli captors. US activist Alex Colston reported that 35 people suffered rib fractures, at least 12 sexual assaults occurred, and detainees were tased and kicked. Israeli National Security Minister Itamar Ben-Gvir posted video of himself taunting handcuffed, kneeling detainees, which drew condemnation even from within Israel's government. Prime Minister Netanyahu rebuked Ben-Gvir and ordered the rapid deportation.

France, Britain, Portugal, Italy, Canada, and other countries summoned Israeli envoys over the treatment of their citizens. Turkey, which was the flotilla's departure point from Marmaris on May 14, condemned Israel's actions and received the deportees for medical processing via Anadolu Agency. The episode reinforces Turkey's role as both a logistical hub for Gaza solidarity efforts and a recipient of diplomatic fallout from the Israel-Gaza conflict.
sections.whyItMatters: Turkey is once again the transit point for flotilla deportees — a role it last played in 2010 after the Mavi Marmara incident. The episode puts Ankara in a delicate position: it must balance domestic and regional sympathy for the flotilla cause with its ongoing diplomatic engagement with both Israel's allies and Iran's adversaries.

sections.heading: Erdogan Tells Trump Iran Issues Can Be Resolved as Mediation Continues
sections.body:
President Erdoğan told President Trump in a Wednesday phone call that he welcomed the extension of the US-Iran ceasefire and believed "contested issues can be resolved." Turkey has been passing messages between Washington, Tehran, and mediators including Pakistan to seek an end to the Iran war, now in its third month.

Erdoğan also discussed the upcoming NATO Summit in Ankara on July 7–8, telling Trump that Turkey was working to ensure the meeting is "a success in every aspect." Zelensky has been invited to attend. Secretary of State Marco Rubio called the summit "one of the more important leaders' summits in the history of NATO" and said allies should brace for Trump's "disappointment" over their refusal to join the war against Tehran.

Erdoğan additionally raised Syria stability — calling it an "important gain" — and warned about the situation in Lebanon, urging steps to prevent further deterioration amid continued Israel-Hezbollah fighting.
sections.whyItMatters: Turkey's Iran mediation gives Erdoğan leverage in his relationship with Washington at a moment when domestic political risk is high. The NATO summit in Ankara, just seven weeks away, is a major diplomatic stage — but hosting it amid a domestic political crackdown and a cratering lira will test Turkey's ability to project stability.

bottomLine: Turkey is in the grip of its most serious political crisis in years. An appeals court's unprecedented decision to annul the main opposition party's leadership election and reinstate a defeated former chairman has no parallel in modern Turkish history — and it immediately sent markets reeling, with the lira hitting record lows and JPMorgan predicting an emergency rate hike to %. The same day, Erdoğan shuttered one of Istanbul's most prominent universities by presidential decree and Turkey's parliament united across partisan lines to demand Gülen's extradition from the US. On the foreign policy front, Turkey continues positioning itself as an indispensable mediator in the Iran conflict and prepares to host the July NATO summit in Ankara — but the gap between its diplomatic ambitions and its domestic instability is widening fast.
sources: Reuters, Detroit News, Daily Sabah, Al Jazeera, Anadolu Agency, TRT World, AP News, Democracy Now, CryptoBriefing, JPMorgan Research, Cyprus Mail, Yahoo News, Kyiv Independent, BBC Turkish
